title: The Scenic Route
author: Helen Carroway
description: When Susan Halliday clears out her late father's beloved old Volvo, she discovers a notebook filled with mysterious routes through the Yorkshire Dales. Following them, she uncovers a secret life of hidden paintings, a family tragedy buried for forty years, and a father she never truly knew — a man who spent his final days quietly leaving beauty behind for strangers to find.

content.text: Three weeks after her father's funeral, Susan Halliday found herself standing in the dim light of his garage, staring at a 1998 Volvo V70 estate in a shade of green that had never existed in nature. The car had been Gerald Halliday's pride and absurdity for twenty-five years. He'd bought it second-hand from a dealer in Skipton who had clearly been desperate to get rid of it, and he had refused to part with it through four MOT failures, two flooded footwells, and a period in 2011 when the passenger door would only open from the outside. Susan's mother, Barbara, had campaigned for its retirement with the persistence of a trade unionist. Gerry had simply patted its bonnet and said, 'She's got another decade in her yet.' He had been wrong about that, as it turned out. He had been wrong about a lot of things, or so Susan was beginning to discover.

Gerry had died on a Wednesday afternoon, in the gardening section of B&Q, holding a bag of tulip bulbs. A massive heart attack, the doctor had said. Quick, at least. Susan's sister, Karen, had pointed out that it was entirely typical of their father to go out buying flowers for other people's gardens. He'd never planted the tulips he bought for his own. He just liked the idea of them. Susan had laughed at that, the hollow, brittle laugh that had become her default setting since the phone call from her mother. Now, standing in the garage with a bin bag and a box of latex gloves, she wasn't laughing. The garage smelled of old oil and damp concrete, and the Volvo sat in the centre of it like a green cathedral, solemn and patient, as if still waiting for Gerry to come back and turn the key.

Susan had volunteered to clear out the car. Karen, who was an accountant in Harrogate and approached all problems with spreadsheets, had volunteered to deal with the house insurance and the solicitor. Barbara, still dazed and deflecting, had volunteered to make tea at irregular intervals. So the Volvo fell to Susan, the elder daughter, the one who had always been good at endings. She opened the driver's door — which groaned like a ship in a storm — and began the archaeology. The footwell was a museum of her father's latter years: Tesco receipts, stray Biros, a crushed packet of Murray Mints, two folded OS maps of the Yorkshire Dales with routes marked in red biro, and, wedged between the seats, a small leather-bound notebook with 'G.H.H.' embossed on the cover in faded gold.

Susan opened the notebook expecting shopping lists or mileage records. Instead, she found something else entirely. The pages were filled with her father's careful, geography-teacher handwriting — she recognised it from years of help-with-homework and postcards from school trips — and they contained routes. Not just routes, but detailed itineraries. 'Kettlewell — park at the river. Walk to the bridge. Tea at Dot's. Check the stone.' Dates beside each entry, stretching back almost two years. 'Grassington — community centre. Leave the painting behind the radiator. Don't be seen.' Susan turned the pages, frowning. 'Settle — hospital waiting room. The one with the blue chairs. Leave the landscape. Small one.' She looked up from the notebook and stared through the windscreen at the concrete wall of the garage. Her father, a man who walked to the paper shop every morning and considered a trip to Otley a significant expedition, had apparently been driving all over the Dales. And he had been leaving things behind.

content.alt: An old green Volvo estate parked in a dimly lit garage
content.caption: The 1998 Volvo V70. Gerry Halliday's faithful companion for twenty-five years.

content.text: Susan sat in the driver's seat — something she hadn't done since she was seventeen and had stalled it six times on a hill in Ilkley — and read the notebook from beginning to end. There were forty-three entries in total, each with a date, a location, a route (invariably the scenic one, never the motorway), and a short note. Some were practical: 'Aysgarth Falls — visitor centre. Leave the river painting on the windowsill.' Others were cryptic: 'Halton Gill — the bridge. Tuesday. Always Tuesday.' And the last entry, dated just two days before he died, read simply: 'Masham — the gallery. Tell Daniel it's time. Make sure Barbara doesn't find out.' Susan closed the notebook and held it against her chest. She could smell her father on the leather — soap and pipe tobacco and the faint, sweet residue of Murray Mints. She sat in the Volvo for a long time, in the cold garage, in the silence, and then she put the key in the ignition. The engine turned over on the second try, coughing and rumbling as if nothing at all had changed.

Susan told no one about the notebook. Not Karen, who would have wanted to analyse it. Not Barbara, who would have wanted to throw it away. Not her estranged husband, Mike, who would have said something well-meaning and useless. Instead, on the following Saturday morning, she packed a flask of coffee, two cheese sandwiches, and the notebook into her own sensible Honda Civic, and drove north. The first entry was dated eighteen months ago and pointed to Kettlewell, a village in Upper Wharfedale that Susan knew only as a place her father had occasionally mentioned with a strange, soft look on his face. The route in the notebook was absurdly indirect — up the A59, through Bolton Abbey, past Burnsall, along the narrow river road that followed the Wharfe like a faithful dog. Gerry had written 'DO NOT USE SATNAV' in block capitals at the top of the page, underlined twice. Susan put her phone in the glovebox and followed his route instead.

It was October, and the Dales were burning. The hillsides were streaked with rust and gold, and the river ran flat and dark between stone walls that had been there longer than any living person. Susan drove with the windows down, letting the cold air rush in, and tried to remember the last time she'd taken a road just because it was beautiful. She couldn't. Her life had become a series of efficient routes: home to school run, school run to office, office to supermarket, supermarket to home. The scenic route was a luxury she'd forgotten existed. Her father had never forgotten. He had lived for back roads and unexpected views, for the village you passed through on the way to somewhere else. 'Life's too short for motorways, Susan,' he'd said a hundred times. She'd always thought it was a quirk. Now, following his handwritten directions through a landscape that seemed to glow with private meaning, she wasn't so sure.

Kettlewell was exactly as she imagined it — a scatter of stone houses, a river bridge, a village green, two pubs, and a small tearoom with a hand-painted sign that read 'Dot's Place.' Susan parked where the notebook instructed — beside the river, under a large ash tree — and walked across the bridge. The water was low, running clear over pale stones, and she stood for a moment watching it, trying to feel whatever it was her father had felt. She felt cold, and slightly foolish, and achingly sad. She pushed open the door of Dot's Place. The tearoom was small and warm, with six tables, a blackboard menu, and a front window that looked directly onto the river. A woman in her sixties with silver hair and flour on her apron looked up from behind the counter. 'Just you, love? Sit anywhere.' Susan chose the corner table — the one by the window, the one her father had noted — and ordered a pot of tea and a scone. Then she took out her phone, found a photo of Gerry, and showed it to the woman. 'I'm looking for this man,' she said. 'My father. Gerald Halliday. I think he used to come here.' The woman — Dot, presumably — set down the teapot and looked at the photo for a long time. Then her face opened into a smile so wide it crinkled her entire face. 'Gerry!' she said. 'Of course I know Gerry. Sits right where you're sitting, every third Tuesday. Pot of tea, fruit scone, extra butter. And the painting, of course.'

Susan's hand tightened around her cup. 'The painting?' Dot nodded, already moving toward the back of the shop. 'He brings them in, see. Little paintings, landscapes, that sort of thing. Leaves them on the windowsill. I put a little price tag on them — ten pounds, fifteen — and they sell within the week. People love them. He never signs them, though. I asked him once and he just smiled and said,

' Susan stared at her. 'My father paints?' Dot laughed. 'Oh, love, your father doesn't just paint. Your father paints the way other people breathe. I've got one left, if you want to see it.' She disappeared into the back and returned with a small canvas, perhaps eight inches by ten. It was a view of the river at Kettlewell, the bridge and the ash tree and the low October light on the water. It was luminous. The colours were rich and layered, applied with a confidence that seemed to belong to a different man entirely from the quiet, hesitant figure Susan had known. On the back, in Gerry's handwriting, was a small note: 'Take this if it makes you smile.' Susan held the painting and felt something shift inside her, a tectonic plate of understanding moving beneath the surface of everything she thought she knew.

chapters.title: Chapter 3 — The Easel in the Attic

content.text: Susan drove home through the darkening afternoon with the painting on the passenger seat, propped against the seatbelt as if it were a fellow traveller. She kept glancing at it, half expecting it to disappear. Her father had been a geography teacher at Modem Comprehensive in Bradford for thirty-two years. He had retired in 2009 and spent his days walking, gardening, listening to Radio 4, and making increasingly elaborate model railways in the spare room. He had been, as far as Susan knew, a man without mystery. And yet here was evidence that he had been living a second life — driving to remote villages, painting landscapes, leaving them behind like offerings. She needed to know more. She needed to find the paintings.

That evening, after Barbara had gone to bed early with a cup of cocoa and a Valium, Susan climbed the pull-down ladder into the attic of her parents' house. The attic was a landscape of memory — boxes of schoolbooks, Karen's old bike, Christmas decorations going back three decades, a broken treadle sewing machine that had belonged to Susan's grandmother. Susan worked systematically, moving boxes aside, peering into dark corners. Behind a stack of National Geographics from the 1990s, she found what she was looking for: a wooden art case, the kind that folded open like a small bureau, its surface dusty but its brass clasps still bright. Inside were oil paints — Winsor & Newton, some of them nearly empty — a set of sable brushes, a palette stained with dried colours, and a stack of small canvases wrapped in tissue paper. Susan unwrapped them one by one. There were fourteen paintings in total. The Dales in every season: the limestone pavement at Malham in snow, the river at Aysgarth in full spate, a dry-stone wall running over a hilltop like a stitch in green cloth. Each one was unsigned but unmistakably the work of the same hand that had painted the Kettlewell river. They were beautiful. They were accomplished. They were the work of someone who had been painting for decades, not months.

content.alt: Paints and brushes in an old wooden art case
content.caption: Gerry's hidden studio. Decades of painting, wrapped in tissue and silence.

content.text: Susan sat on the attic floor surrounded by paintings and tried to piece together what she knew. Gerry had painted — extensively, seriously — and then stopped. When? And why? She thought back to her childhood, looking for clues. There had been an easel in the dining room when she was very small. She was almost sure of it. A smell of linseed oil and turpentine that her mother had complained about, said it gave her headaches. And then, at some point, the easel had disappeared. The smell had gone. Gerry had taken up model railways instead. Susan had been too young to notice the change, or to question it. Now, sitting in the dust and the dim light, she felt the familiar heat of anger — not at her father, who had clearly been doing something he loved in secret, at enormous trouble and expense — but at her mother, who had almost certainly been the reason he'd stopped.

The next morning, Susan carried one of the paintings downstairs and set it on the kitchen table while Barbara was making toast. 'Mum, did you know Dad was painting again?' Barbara looked at the canvas — a view of Semer Water in evening light — and her face did something complicated. A flicker of surprise, then recognition, then a tightening around the mouth that Susan knew from childhood as the expression that preceded the word 'nonsense.' 'Where did you find that?' Barbara asked. 'In the attic. There are fourteen more. And he's been leaving them in cafes and community centres all over the Dales. Did you know?' Barbara put down her knife. 'He gave that up years ago,' she said. 'When you were little. I told him it was a waste of time and money. He had a family to provide for. Teaching was his job, not daubing on canvas.' Susan felt the anger rise. 'He never stopped, Mum. He just stopped telling you.' Barbara's eyes went hard, then soft, then something else — something that looked, uncomfortably, like guilt. 'Well,' she said, picking up her toast. 'I didn't know. And I don't see what difference it makes now.' Susan looked at her mother — seventy-one years old, stubborn, sharp, and as impenetrable as the dry-stone walls in Gerry's paintings — and decided not to argue. She packed the painting back into the car and drove to Settle, where the notebook said her father had left a landscape in a hospital waiting room with blue chairs.

Settle's community hospital was a modest building on the edge of town, brick and glass, with a car park that smelled of rain. Susan walked into the waiting room and immediately saw the blue chairs her father had described — a row of moulded plastic seats in a shade of blue that was presumably chosen for its calming properties but in fact induced a vague nausea. The room was empty except for an elderly man reading a Western paperback and a woman in a nurse's uniform sorting paperwork at the reception desk. Susan looked at the walls. There were three paintings hanging: a print of sunflowers, a watercolour of the sea, and — in the corner, above a radiator — a small oil painting of a barn owl perched on a gatepost, its eyes bright against a winter sky. Susan walked closer. It was the same style, the same confident brushwork, the same unsigned back. But this one had a small label beside it, written in neat handwriting: 'Anonymous donation. Please enjoy.' She asked the nurse about it. 'Oh, that one? A gentleman brought it in about a year ago. Wouldn't leave his name. Said he wanted to brighten the room up. And he was right — it does. Patients love it. We've had people ask if they can buy it.' Susan took a photo of the painting and added it to the growing collection on her phone. Four locations so far: Kettlewell, Settle, Grassington, and now Aysgarth. Her father had been running a secret art distribution network across the Yorkshire Dales, and no one in his family had known.

Over the following week, Susan visited every location in the notebook. She drove to Grassington Community Centre, where a painting of a hay meadow in summer hung above the reception desk. She went to the care home in Skipton, where a cheerful nurse named Priya showed her a landscape of Malham Cove that hung in the residents' day room. 'A lovely man brought it,' Priya said. 'Older gentleman, very polite. He said his wife didn't approve of his hobby and he wanted the paintings to be somewhere they'd be appreciated.' Susan felt each visit like a small wound. At every stop, someone remembered Gerry — not as a sad old man with a heart condition, but as a quiet, kind presence who arrived with a painting and left without fuss. He had, it seemed, been building a gallery without walls, scattering his work across the Dales like seeds, and the flowers had grown without him ever seeing them bloom.

At the care home, Priya gave Susan something else. 'He left this with me,' she said, handing over a sealed envelope. 'Said if anyone ever came asking about the paintings, I should give it to them.' Susan opened it in the car park, her hands trembling. Inside was a single sheet of paper, folded once, with Gerry's handwriting. 'Dear whoever finds this,' it read. 'If you're reading this, I'm probably dead, and someone has finally noticed the paintings. I'm sorry for the secrecy. Barbara never approved, and it was easier to be quiet than to fight. But I want you to know that painting was the thing that made me feel most alive, apart from my daughters, and I couldn't stop any more than I could stop breathing. I started again after my heart scare in 2019. Dr Patel said I should do something that made me happy. So I did. If you're my Susan or my Karen, don't be angry with your mother. She did what she thought was right. And don't be sad for me. I had seventy-three good years, and I spent the last two doing exactly what I wanted. — Dad.'

Susan read the letter three times, then sat in her car in the Skipton care home car park and cried. She cried for the father who had hidden the best part of himself for forty years. She cried for the mother who had demanded he hide it. She cried for herself, for Karen, for all the years they could have known their father as he truly was — a man who saw the world in colour and light and wanted to share it. When she finally drove home, she took the scenic route. Through the Dales in late October, with the hills on fire and the sky a pale, hard blue. She drove past the places her father had painted, and she saw them differently now — not just landscape, but the raw material of a secret passion, captured in oil by a man who had been, in his own quiet way, an artist all along.

chapters.title: Chapter 5 — The Bridge at Halton Gill

content.text: There was one entry in the notebook that Susan had been avoiding. It appeared several times across the eighteen months of routes, always the same: 'Halton Gill — the bridge. Tuesday.' No mention of paintings, no mention of leaving anything behind. Just the bridge, every month, without fail. Susan looked up Halton Gill on her father's OS map — the one with the red biro routes — and found it marked with a small star. It was a tiny hamlet at the head of Littondale, a side valley off Wharfedale, remote even by Dales standards. A church, a few farms, a river, and a bridge. Why would her father drive an hour and a half every month to visit a bridge in the middle of nowhere? Susan decided it was time to find out.

She drove on a Tuesday, as the notebook instructed. The route took her through Wharfedale, past Grassington and Kettlewell, then left up the narrow road into Littondale. The valley was narrower than Wharfedale, enclosed by high fells that rose steeply on either side, their slopes patched with drystone walls and scattered sheep. Halton Gill appeared around a bend — a cluster of stone buildings, a small church with a square tower, and, spanning the river, a single-arched stone bridge that was old enough to have a gentle hump in its back. Susan parked beside the church and walked to the bridge. It was quiet — the kind of quiet that feels like a physical presence. The river ran beneath the arch in a series of small, clear steps, and the banks were thick with moss and late ferns. Susan stood in the centre of the bridge and looked down at the water. She felt nothing. Or rather, she felt the absence of something — a space where meaning should have been.

Then she noticed the garden. On the downstream side of the bridge, tucked between the stone wall and the riverbank, was a small patch of cultivated ground. Wildflowers — late-flowering ones, hardy enough for October — grew in careful clusters: sedum, asters, a few stubborn roses. A low slate border held the soil in place, and in the centre, a simple wooden bench had been installed, its wood weathered but not yet grey. Susan knelt beside it and found, set into the ground at the bench's foot, a small brass plaque. She wiped the dirt from its surface and read the inscription: 'For Michael. 1955–1984. Take the scenic route, brother.' Michael. Susan sat back on her heels. She had never heard this name. There was no Michael in the family, as far as she knew. Her father had been an only child — or so he'd always said. She pulled out her phone and called Karen. 'Do we have an uncle called Michael?' she asked. 'What? No. Dad was an only child. Why?' Susan read the plaque to her. Karen was silent for a long time. 'I don't know who that is, Susan. Dad never mentioned anyone called Michael.' Susan called her mother. Barbara answered on the fourth ring, her voice flat and guarded. 'Mum, who is Michael?' Susan said. There was a silence so long that Susan thought the line had gone dead. Then Barbara said, in a voice she had never heard her mother use before — small, stripped, raw — 'Where did you find that name?'

content.alt: An old stone bridge over a clear river in a quiet valley
content.caption: The bridge at Halton Gill. A memorial, and a secret held for forty years.

content.text: Barbara came to Halton Gill the next day. She sat on the bench beside the wildflowers and looked at the river and didn't speak for twenty minutes. Susan sat beside her and waited. When Barbara finally spoke, her voice was low and careful, as if she were handling something fragile. 'Michael was your father's younger brother. He died here, on this bridge, in 1984. A car accident. Gerry was driving.' Susan stared at her. 'Dad was driving? He never said — he never told us he had a brother.' Barbara nodded slowly. 'He couldn't talk about it. It broke something in him. Michael was twenty-nine. He was visiting from London. Gerry went to pick him up from the station in Skipton, and on the way back, in the rain, the car went off the bridge. Gerry walked away with a broken arm. Michael didn't walk away at all.' Barbara paused, her hands folded tightly in her lap. 'Gerry gave up painting after that. They used to paint together — Michael was an art student in London, and Gerry used to go up and visit his exhibitions. After the accident, Gerry put everything away. The paints, the brushes, the easel. I thought he was moving on. I didn't know he was just burying it.' Susan felt the ground shift again, another tectonic plate. Her father hadn't just been hiding a hobby. He'd been hiding a grief so enormous it had reshaped the entire landscape of his life.

'He came here every month,' Susan said softly. 'For two years. Maybe longer.' Barbara looked at the wildflowers, the slate border, the bench. 'I didn't know any of this,' she said. 'He never told me. He just said he was going for a drive.' Susan thought about all the Sunday mornings Gerry had left the house with a Thermos and a sandwich, saying he was going to explore the back roads. She'd imagined him parked at a viewpoint, reading the paper, enjoying the peace. Instead, he'd been here, tending a grave, sitting with his dead brother, and then driving on to paint the hills that Michael would never see. 'He was saying goodbye,' Susan said. 'Or maybe he was saying sorry.' Barbara reached out and gripped Susan's hand, hard. 'He had nothing to be sorry for. It was an accident. The road was wet. The car was old. But Gerry never believed that. He carried it like a stone around his neck for forty years.' They sat together on the bench by the bridge, mother and daughter, and the river ran on beneath them, indifferent and eternal.

The final entry in the notebook — 'Masham — the gallery. Tell Daniel it's time. Make sure Barbara doesn't find out' — took Susan to a small market town on the edge of the Dales. Masham was famous for its breweries and its sheep fair, not for its art scene, but the notebook directed her to a converted stable behind a stone building on the market square. A hand-painted sign above the door read 'The Gallery at No. 9.' Susan pushed it open and found herself in a single, bright room with whitewashed walls, a polished wooden floor, and perhaps twenty paintings hung in careful arrangement. A man in his fifties — tall, bearded, with paint-stained fingers and wire-rimmed glasses — looked up from a desk in the corner. 'Can I help you?' Susan took a breath. 'I'm looking for Daniel. My father was Gerald Halliday. He told me to come.' The man stood up so quickly his chair scraped the floor. 'Gerry's daughter? Good God. I've been waiting. I've been waiting since — well, since I heard.' He came around the desk and took both her hands. 'I'm Daniel Foster. Your father was — he was the most extraordinary thing that happened to this gallery. And I mean that sincerely.'

Daniel led her to the back wall of the gallery, where a section had been partitioned off with a cream curtain. 'I was supposed to unveil this three weeks ago,' he said. 'Your father was supposed to be here. He'd been planning it for months.' He pulled back the curtain. Susan gasped. The wall held twelve paintings — the largest ones Gerry had ever done, Daniel said, each measuring two feet by three. They were a series: the Yorkshire Dales through the seasons, from the first snow at Hawes to the last light at Kilnsey Crag. Each was luminous, layered, rich with the kind of emotion that only comes from a lifetime of looking. And each was signed, in the corner, with a name Susan had never seen: 'G. H. Dale.' 'He chose the pseudonym himself,' Daniel explained. 'Dale for the Dales, he said. He was terrified your mother would find out. He said she'd made him stop once and he couldn't go through that again. So he painted in secret, drove the paintings here in the boot of that awful green Volvo, and we arranged everything by phone. He was going to come to the opening. We had it all planned — the 14th of November, a Saturday. Wine, cheese, a speech. He was so nervous about the speech he'd written it out six times.'

Susan walked along the wall, studying each painting. They were extraordinary. Not just competent, not just pleasant, but genuinely moving — the work of someone who had spent decades absorbing a landscape and translating it into colour. How could she not have known? How could her father have carried this talent, this passion, this entire identity, hidden inside his quiet, ordinary life? She stopped at the final painting. It was the bridge at Halton Gill, but painted in colours that had nothing to do with grief. The stone was warm gold, the river a bright, dancing blue-green, the banks blazing with wildflowers. It was a place of joy, not of loss. On the back, in Gerry's handwriting: 'For Michael, who would have loved these hills. Forgive yourself, old man. He would have told you to.' Susan turned to Daniel. 'He knew he was dying,' she said. It wasn't a question. Daniel nodded. 'He came in two days before he died. He looked grey, Susan. He told me his doctor had given him a warning — six months, maybe less, if he was lucky. His heart. He said he wanted to make sure everything was ready. He said he'd wasted too many years being afraid, and he wasn't going to waste what was left.' Daniel paused, adjusting his glasses. 'He also said to tell you — specifically you, not Karen, you — that he was sorry he never showed you. He said you would have understood.'

Susan pressed her hand against her mouth. 'I would have,' she whispered. 'I would have understood.' Daniel put a hand on her shoulder. 'I know. I think he knew that too, in the end. That's why he wrote the note. That's why he left the notebook in the car. He wanted you to find it. He trusted you to follow the routes.' Susan looked around the gallery — the whitewashed walls, the careful lighting, the twelve paintings that represented her father's secret heart — and made a decision. 'The exhibition,' she said. 'The 14th of November. Can we still do it?' Daniel smiled. 'I was hoping you'd say that.'

Susan and Karen spent the next two weeks organising the exhibition. Karen, true to form, handled logistics — invitations, catering, press releases — with the efficiency of a military campaign. Susan handled the emotional labour, which was messier and harder. She called every person who had known Gerry through his secret life: Dot from the tearoom, Priya from the care home, the receptionist at the Settle hospital, the community centre manager in Grassington. She invited them all. She also, after much deliberation, told her mother everything. Barbara listened in silence, sitting at the kitchen table with her hands folded, her face unreadable. When Susan finished, Barbara was quiet for a long time. Then she said, 'I was wrong. I knew it then, and I did it anyway. I thought if he stopped painting, he'd stop thinking about Michael. I thought it would make him happy — a normal life, a family, a steady job. But he was never happy, was he? Not really. Not until the end.' Susan wanted to say something sharp, something that would make her mother feel the weight of what she'd done. But she looked at Barbara — old, tired, grieving a husband she'd loved imperfectly and controlled too much — and she said, 'He was happy at the end, Mum. That's what matters. He found his way back to it.' Barbara wiped her eyes with the back of her hand and nodded. 'The exhibition,' she said. 'I'd like to come. If that's all right.'

The 14th of November was a cold, bright Saturday. The Gallery at No. 9 in Masham was packed by two o'clock. Susan had expected perhaps thirty people — a few friends, some curious locals. Instead, over a hundred came. Word had spread, as word does in small communities, and people arrived from across the Dales: farmers and shopkeepers and nurses and teachers, people who had known Gerry only as the quiet man who left paintings behind, and people who had known him all his life and had never guessed. Dot brought scones. Priya brought two residents from the care home in wheelchairs. Daniel gave a speech that made everyone cry. And Barbara — Barbara, who had spent fifty years telling her husband to be sensible, to be practical, to stop wasting time — stood in front of the painting of Halton Gill bridge and looked at it for a very long time. 'They're beautiful,' she said finally, in a voice that cracked. 'He was talented. I was wrong to stop him. I was so wrong.' Karen put an arm around her. Susan stood back and watched the room — the strangers and the friends, the paintings and the tears — and felt something she hadn't felt since her father died: gratitude.

content.alt: A small art gallery with paintings on white walls and warm lighting
content.caption: The Gallery at No. 9, Masham. Gerry Halliday's first — and last — exhibition.

content.text: After the exhibition, Susan drove home the long way. Not through the main roads, not through the efficient A-roads that would have gotten her home in forty minutes, but through the Dales, following the routes in her father's notebook one last time. She drove through Kettlewell as the sun went down, the river dark and the ash tree bare. She drove through Grassington, where the community centre lights were on and someone was setting out chairs for a meeting. She drove up Littondale to Halton Gill, where the bridge was a pale arch in the dusk and the wildflowers had died back for winter but the bench remained, solid and patient. She stopped and sat on the bench and listened to the river. 'I found them, Dad,' she said quietly. 'The paintings. The gallery. All of it. I understand.' The river said nothing, but the silence felt like an answer.

When Susan arrived home, her teenage son, Jack, was on the sofa playing a video game. He looked up as she came in. 'Where've you been, Mum? You said you'd be back hours ago.' Susan looked at him — sixteen, lanky, irritated in the way only teenagers can be — and smiled. 'I took the scenic route,' she said. Jack rolled his eyes. 'Why would you do that? It takes twice as long.' Susan sat down beside him and turned off the console. 'Because life's too short for motorways,' she said. 'Your grandfather used to say that.' Jack looked at her blankly. 'Grandad used to say a lot of things. He was well weird.' Susan laughed — a real laugh, full and warm, the kind she hadn't managed in weeks. 'Yes,' she said. 'He was. And I'm going to tell you all about it. Every single detour.' Jack groaned, but he didn't get up. He stayed on the sofa, and Susan began to talk — about the green Volvo, the hidden paintings, the bridge at Halton Gill, and the quiet, extraordinary man who had been her father. Outside, the November dark pressed against the windows, and the kettle clicked off in the kitchen, and somewhere in the Yorkshire Dales, a small painting of a barn owl hung above a radiator in a hospital waiting room, unsigned and unclaimed, bringing comfort to strangers who would never know the name of the man who made it. Life was too short for motorways. Susan intended to take the scenic route from now on.
